title: #architecture: 1738703321
preview:
Talia Benson: Hey folks — need to schedule a design review for the delivery semantics ADR (audit export). This is the one covering at-least-once, idempotency, checkpointing, replay/backfill. Can we get 45m this week? Target attendees: @Ethan Park @Marcus Lin @Kevin Osei @Sean Gallagher @Rafael Mendes @Logan Wright @Jordan Lee. Link: https://confluence.redwoodinference.com/display/ARCH/adr-audit-export-delivery-semantics-and-idempotency (draft)

Ethan Park: +1. We should lock this before we finalize the control plane config API behavior. I can do Thu 11-12 PT or Fri 2-3 PT.

Marcus Lin: I’m in Thu 11 PT. Main question from Sec side: are we *explicitly* saying at-least-once and pushing dedup downstream? We need crisp language for customers + SOC2 evidence.

Kevin Osei: Same. Also need to be super clear on retention vs export. “Guaranteed delivery” gets interpreted as “exactly-once” and “no gaps”, which we can’t promise in outages w/ throttling.

Sean Gallagher: From SRE POV: exactly-once is basically a trap here unless we’re willing to build transactional sinks + state per destination at scale. Prefer at-least-once with bounded replay + tooling. Also need safety valves when downstream is hard-down (auto-pause, max lag).

Rafael Mendes: Agree. If we do exactly-once semantics at the exporter level, we’ll be fighting duplicates *and* missing events forever. At-least-once + stable `event_id` + clear guidance that SIEM should dedup is the sane path.

Logan Wright: Observability angle: at-least-once is easier to reason about in metrics. We can expose lag, retry counts, DLQ rates. Exactly-once claims will generate tickets when customers see duplicates in Splunk.

Jordan Lee: Question: how do we define idempotency key? `event_id` is per event (globally unique) — but exporters will batch. Do we ever need a `delivery_id` per destination attempt?

Benji Okafor: Draft ADR says: primary dedup key is `event_id` (uuid v7-ish) emitted at audit event creation time. Exporter retries can send duplicates; downstream should dedup on `event_id`. For S3, we’re writing files (partitioned) so dedup is more “consumer dedup” unless they ingest into something.

Marcus Lin: I’m ok with `event_id` but we must ensure it’s stable + non-PII and exists for *all* events. Also: how do we handle events emitted pre-v2 schema? Are we going to backfill IDs or just say beta is v2 only?

Ethan Park: Beta will require audit schema v2 (ENG-5127). We can do a compatibility mapping but exporter should only export events that have `event_id`. Otherwise we’ll create non-deduppable events.

Kevin Osei: That’s fine but we need to be explicit in beta docs: “only v2 events exported; older events not backfilled.” Also define backfill window clearly.

Sean Gallagher: Another angle: we should avoid “exactly once” anywhere in docs/UI. It’s not even true for Splunk HEC unless we implement an ack + checkpoint at event granularity and handle every edge case.

Jordan Lee: What if we do: exporter persists checkpoint as “max event_time seen” and “last exported event_id”? That still has ordering assumptions.

Benji Okafor: Current plan is checkpoint by monotonically increasing cursor from the audit store (think `event_seq` / `insert_id`), not `event_time`. Time is not safe for cursoring. ADR mentions “cursor is storage-assigned, strictly increasing per tenant/workspace”.

Rafael Mendes: Yes please. Using timestamps for cursoring will cause gaps during clock skew / delayed ingestion.

Logan Wright: For Splunk: HEC returns 200 even if events are partially accepted? (depends). We should clarify what we consider “delivered”. If we treat HTTP 200 as success for the batch, duplicates will be possible on retry but acceptable.

Marcus Lin: That’s the customer confusion point. We need the language: “at-least-once to destination endpoint; customer should deduplicate using `event_id`.” Plus: “export can be paused during prolonged downstream outage; audit log in Redwood remains source of truth.”

Kevin Osei: +1. Also need explicit mention of DLQ and operator-assisted replay: “failed batches may be queued for replay; replay can result in duplicates.”

Connor O'Brien: On rollout mechanics: we also need a decision on what happens if a config changes while exporter is mid-stream. Do we reset checkpoint? pause? This impacts “duplicates vs missing”.

Ethan Park: Draft behavior: config changes do NOT reset checkpoint by default. If filters/destination change, we continue from last cursor. If customer wants backfill, they set `backfill_window` (bounded). That might re-send older events = duplicates. UI must warn.

Sean Gallagher: Please add guardrails: max backfill window in beta (e.g., 7d) + rate limit replays. Otherwise someone will set 90d and nuke the exporter.

Talia Benson: Ok sounds like the meeting needs to cover:
1) Exactly-once vs at-least-once wording (customer-facing)
2) Cursor/checkpoint model (seq vs time)
3) Replay/backfill semantics + bounds
4) Destination-specific ack semantics (Splunk/Datadog/S3)
5) Safety valves (auto-pause / max lag)

Talia Benson: I’ll schedule Thu 11:00–11:45 PT. If you can’t make it, drop comments in ADR.

Google Calendar: Event created: “ADR Review: Audit Export Delivery Semantics + Idempotency” Thu Feb 6, 11:00–11:45am PT. Guests: Talia Benson, Ethan Park, Marcus Lin, Kevin Osei, Sean Gallagher, Rafael Mendes, Logan Wright, Jordan Lee, Benji Okafor, Connor O'Brien. Location: https://zoom.us/j/987654321

Marcus Lin: Thanks. I’ll add comments tonight on “what we can/can’t claim” for compliance.

Kevin Osei: I’ll also propose FAQ language: “at-least-once, may contain duplicates; use `event_id` for dedup.”

Sean Gallagher: I’ll review the failure modes section and ensure we’re not implicitly promising no duplicates.

Logan Wright: I’ll come with metrics/alerts we need to validate the semantics (lag, retry, DLQ depth).

Ethan Park: Great. Also Jordan/Benji can we make sure ADR calls out idempotency expectations per destination? Splunk/Datadog can dedup in pipeline; S3 is just object store.

Jordan Lee: yep, will add a note: S3 delivery is “at-least-once object writes” (possible duplicate objects) and consumer should handle. We should also use deterministic object keying where possible to reduce duplicates.

Benji Okafor: careful: deterministic keys can lead to overwrite = data loss if partial writes. Prefer unique keys + manifest? We can debate in the review.

Rafael Mendes: +1, overwrites are scary. Unique objects + checkpoint in our DB is safer; duplicates are OK.

Talia Benson: Perfect. See you Thu.

title: #eng-platform: 1738451190
preview:
Logan Wright: Question for the group re experiment metrics dims: what are we using as `route_id`? Right now I’m seeing a mix of raw path (`/v1/accounts/123/chat`) and templated (`/v1/accounts/{account_id}/chat`) depending on where the span is created.

Caleb Johnson: +1. In OTel we currently tag `http.route` when we have it, but gateway sometimes only has `http.target` (raw) unless the route matcher passes template down.

Allison Grant: Raw path is going to explode cardinality. We should not be emitting per-request metrics with raw path as a label, especially for anything under customers’ dynamic prefixes.

Jada Williams: Agree. For Console “Route Experiments” we already have a concept of “route” (policy route). That should map to a stable identifier, not raw URL.

Jordan Lee: From policy engine POV, we evaluate experiments after route match. We can attach a stable `route_uuid` (from route config) into request context. That’s the cleanest.

Mina Haddad: There’s also the usability angle: Console wants to show something human readable. If we only emit UUID, dashboards are useless without a join.

Mei Lin: For analytics joins are fine (we already join route table for rollouts views), but for oncall Grafana it’s painful if all you see is UUIDs.

Rafael Mendes: Oncall perspective: I want “which endpoint is burning” immediately. Route template is pretty good for that. Raw path is worst.

Logan Wright: So maybe we emit BOTH `route_id` (stable) and `route_template` (low-cardinality string)?

Allison Grant: Caution: “route_template” is only low-cardinality if it’s actually the *template*. If we accidentally pass raw, we’re back to cardinality hell. Needs normalization + validation.

Caleb Johnson: We can implement a guard: if `route_template` contains a long numeric segment or UUID-looking segment, drop/replace with `_` or just don’t set the label.

Dylan Brooks: What does Console use today for rollouts dashboards? Is it template or internal ID?

Mei Lin: Console rollouts uses internal route record (UUID) and then displays the saved “Route name” / “Path pattern” from config. The metrics pipeline currently keys by UUID for rollouts.

Jada Williams: For experiments we should align with that: canonical dimension is `route_id` (UUID) and optionally `route_pattern` for readability.

Jordan Lee: Implementation detail: gateway/router should set `route_id` as soon as route is matched (before experiment evaluator). Then experiment evaluator adds `{experiment_id, variant_id}`. That avoids re-parsing URL.

Logan Wright: What about customers who define multiple routes with same template but different methods/hosts? E.g. `POST /v1/chat/completions` vs `GET /v1/chat/completions`.

Caleb Johnson: Yeah, `http.route` alone doesn’t include method. We should include method either in the template string or as another label.

Allison Grant: Please don’t add more labels unless we have to. But method is tiny-cardinality (GET/POST/etc) so it’s acceptable. Host maybe not.

Rafael Mendes: Method matters for troubleshooting too. I’d be fine with `http.method` staying as-is, and `route_template` being path-only.

Mina Haddad: Another wrinkle: some customers mount routes with tenant prefixes (`/customer/acme/...`). Even if templated, that still adds cardinality per tenant.

Jada Williams: Those should not be treated as “route templates” from our perspective. Our route configs should use normalized patterns (we don’t recommend per-tenant path prefixes; they should use headers). But reality exists.

Allison Grant: We should define normalization rules:
- Prefer route config ID (UUID) as the canonical `route_id`
- For display labels, use route’s configured pattern *after* normalization (replace path params with `{param}` tokens)
- If the configured pattern itself is high-cardinality (contains literal tenant strings), we still emit route_id, and either (a) don’t emit route_template or (b) emit a coarse label like `custom_route`.

Logan Wright: Makes sense. But for Grafana, without route_template we lose quick triage.

Mei Lin: Could add a small lookup panel or a runbook link: “How to map route_id to route name in Console” / internal tool. But that’s slower.

Caleb Johnson: Another option: emit `route_group` derived from registry, like `chat-completions`, `embeddings`, etc. That’s stable + useful.

Dylan Brooks: Console already has “Route name” (user-provided) and “Endpoint” (system). We could encourage naming best practices in the wizard.

Rafael Mendes: +1. Oncall would rather have `route_name` than a path string anyway.

Jada Williams: Can we actually emit `route_name`? That’s user-controlled and might be long / PII / weird chars.

Allison Grant: Please no user-controlled freeform strings in metric labels.

Jordan Lee: From router we can safely emit:
- `route_id` (UUID)
- `route_pattern` (system-generated canonical pattern) if it’s from our registry, not user-supplied
- `http.method`

Logan Wright: OK, so where do we get `route_pattern`? Gateway match rules already know the configured pattern, right?

Caleb Johnson: Today: sometimes. In the API gateway we have it, but in some private deployments we’re missing the compiled route matcher metadata in the span exporter.

Mina Haddad: For hosted/dedicated we can plumb it through request context (same place we put experiment ids). For private we might need a fallback.

Allison Grant: Fallback should be: only emit `route_id` and method; do NOT try to derive from raw path.

Rafael Mendes: +1000. Deriving from raw path is how we got the cardinality spike last quarter.

Logan Wright: What about traces? Can we put raw path in traces (since sampled) and keep metrics normalized?

Caleb Johnson: Traces already have `http.target` (raw) in many cases. That’s OK because tracing backend can handle it better + sampling. The big risk is metrics labels.

Allison Grant: Exactly. Metrics: strict. Traces/logs: more flexible.

Dylan Brooks: So the concrete ask: define what we call it. `route_id` vs `route` vs `http.route`?

Logan Wright: ADR-048 uses `route_id` as canonical dimension. We can follow that and set `route_id` to UUID, and optionally set `http.route` to the pattern.

Jordan Lee: Works. In policy engine we can set `route_id` in context as `routing.route_id` and exporter maps to `route_id` tag.

Mei Lin: For analytics dataset, we’ll also store `route_id` and join to route registry to display name/pattern in Console results.

Rafael Mendes: Can we also update the runbook to call out “don’t use raw path as route id” and add a dashboard panel to show top route_ids by traffic so you can click into Console?

Allison Grant: Yes, and we should add an alert for “route_template cardinality anomaly” (e.g. too many distinct values) if we end up emitting template.

Caleb Johnson: I can add a sanity check in the OTel exporter: if `http.route` value count exceeds threshold in a window, drop `http.route` label and emit a counter like `route_label_dropped=1`.

Logan Wright: Love it. That also gives us a signal for misconfig.

Mina Haddad: One more: route normalization across regions. If route_id is UUID from config, it’s consistent across regions right?

Jordan Lee: Yep, config is global, route IDs are stable. That’s why UUID is best.

Allison Grant: Decision proposal:
1) Canonical dimension for experiments + metrics attribution: `route_id` (UUID from route config/registry)
2) Emit `http.route` ONLY when we have a system-generated canonical pattern; never derive it from raw request path
3) Keep `http.target` / raw path in traces/logs only
4) Include `http.method` as separate low-cardinality label
5) Add exporter guardrails to prevent accidental high-cardinality `http.route`

Dylan Brooks: +1

Rafael Mendes: +1

Mei Lin: +1

Caleb Johnson: +1, I’ll take the exporter guardrail action item.

Logan Wright: +1. I’ll update the metrics taxonomy doc + ping Console to make sure results view joins on route_id and shows pattern/name.

Jada Williams: I’ll make sure router sets `route_id` before experiment evaluation and that we propagate it through retries.

Allison Grant: Cool. I’ll summarize in the standards doc + link in the eng-sre thread where cardinality concerns came up.

title: #eng-platform: 1738186033
preview:
Jada Williams: Need to lock default guardrail thresholds for route experiments (latency/error/cost) before we freeze Console copy. What are we comfortable shipping as defaults for beta + GA?

Paula Mendes: +1. We should pick defaults that are safe (catch real regressions) but not so tight they trigger constantly, esp on low traffic routes.

Sean Gallagher: From SRE POV, defaults must be conservative + predictable. Also we need to be explicit: is this soft-stop only by default? (banner + notify), and hard-stop is opt-in / behind kill switch.

Jada Williams: Yes. Per the earlier thread, default behavior = soft-stop + notification. Hard-stop available but opt-in.

Allison Grant: For latency, I’d suggest p99 + p95. p50 is too noisy. Also recommend comparing variant vs control within same window (not historical baseline) so we don’t conflate daily traffic mix changes.

Logan Wright: Telemetry can support p50/p95/p99 per {route, experiment_id, variant_id, region}. But for guardrails, we should keep it to p95/p99 to reduce confusion.

Nadia Rahman: Cost guardrail definition: cost per 1K output tokens? Or cost per request? Token mix changes can make cost/request misleading.

Paula Mendes: Product wants “Cost / 1K tokens” in the UI (and “$ / request” as secondary). Guardrail should probably use cost per token (or per 1K tokens) so it’s stable across prompt size.

Jordan Lee: Note for routing: if we soft-stop, we set experiment state=degraded but routing stays the same unless operator stops. If we hard-stop, evaluator should remove experiment from consideration (fall back to baseline policy). Need thresholds that won’t flap.

Sean Gallagher: Agree. Also we need minimum sample size gating for guardrails; otherwise a handful of errors can trip error-rate threshold on tiny volume.

Rafael Mendes: Draft proposal (based on what we’ve used internally for canaries):
- Latency guardrail: trigger if variant p99 is > (control p99 * 1.25) OR +250ms absolute (whichever is larger) over a 10m window.
- Secondary latency: p95 > control p95 * 1.20 OR +150ms absolute.
- Error guardrail: trigger if 5xx rate (or “server_error”) is > control by +0.5% absolute AND >1.5x relative over 10m.
- Cost guardrail: cost per 1K tokens > control by +15% over 30m (longer window).
- Requires min N: at least 200 requests per variant in window, and at least 20 errors total for error guardrail.
Thoughts?

Marissa Cole: For Console, we need something we can explain in 1-2 sentences. The “whichever larger” logic is ok but might be confusing. Could we simplify to relative deltas only?

Sean Gallagher: Pure relative deltas are dangerous when control is already very low (e.g., p99 80ms). +25% becomes +20ms which is noise. Absolute floor helps.

Dylan Brooks: UX-wise, we can show “Trigger when p99 is 25% slower (min +250ms)” in tooltip. That’s manageable.

Allison Grant: +1 for absolute floors. Also please include attribution drop rate guardrail? If variant attribution is missing we’ll get weird numbers + angry users.

Logan Wright: That’s more of an experiment health/system guardrail than variant regression. But we can include a separate “data quality” banner: if attribution drop > X% we mark results as unreliable.

Paula Mendes: Let’s keep defaults for experiment guardrails focused on: latency / error / cost. Separate health checks for “config propagation lag” + “attribution drop rate” live in health section.

Felix Schneider: On customization: do we allow per-route overrides for defaults? (e.g., routes with long-running tool calls will have huge p99). I’m worried about a single global default being wrong for some customers.

Jada Williams: Plan was: start with defaults, allow override per experiment (advanced section) + allow per route template (later). But we can ship per-route override in GA if it’s not too much backend work.

Jordan Lee: Backend can support precedence: per-experiment guardrail config overrides route defaults; route defaults override global defaults. But we need to store route defaults in the same policy config model; otherwise it’s messy.

Rafael Mendes: If we don’t have per-route defaults in v1, at least allow per-experiment override + save preset templates (“latency sensitive”, “cost sensitive”).

Nadia Rahman: For cost guardrail window: 30m is safer. Cost signals are spiky (batching/caching) and can be skewed by a few huge prompts. Also define which cost: GPU + platform? Or total billed? We should use the same cost metric Console shows.

Logan Wright: We have “estimated_unit_cost_usd_per_1k_tokens” from the cost attribution pipeline. That’s consistent + available near real-time. Billing numbers lag.

Marissa Cole: Ok, summarizing what I think we’re aligned on:
1) Default guardrail behavior = soft-stop + notifications.
2) Default thresholds:
- p99 latency: 25% regression vs control with absolute floor +250ms (10m window, min 200 req/variant)
- p95 latency: 20% regression vs control with absolute floor +150ms (10m window, min 200 req/variant)
- 5xx error rate: +0.5% absolute AND 1.5x relative vs control (10m window, min 200 req/variant; require >=20 errors to trip)
- Cost per 1K tokens: +15% vs control (30m window, min 200 req/variant)
3) Results reliability banners (not “stop” guardrails): attribution drop rate + propagation lag.
4) Customization: allow per-experiment override in v1; route-level defaults optional (defer if too big).
Can someone confirm / adjust before we update PRD + Console strings?

Sean Gallagher: Confirmed from SRE. Please add: hard-stop remains kill-switchable + opt-in, and guardrail evaluation should require 2 consecutive windows to trigger (reduce flapping).

Paula Mendes: +1 on 2 consecutive windows for defaults. That will read as “sustained regression.”

Jada Williams: Works. I’ll update the guardrail evaluator default to require 2 windows (i.e., 20m for p95/p99/error; 60m for cost) unless operator config changes it.

Dylan Brooks: I’ll sync with Felix on UI copy/tooltips and make sure advanced override doesn’t overwhelm the wizard.

Allison Grant: Please make sure we log the computed deltas + sample sizes into the audit/event stream when a guardrail triggers. Makes oncall triage way faster.

Rafael Mendes: Noted. I’ll add to runbook + ensure alert payload includes: metric, threshold, window, req count, control value, variant value, delta.

Marissa Cole: Great. I’ll post the decision in #product too + update beta enablement doc. :thumbsup:

title: #eng-oncall: 1738096504
preview:
Sean Gallagher: Heads up: automated restore drill in staging failed again. This is the scheduled "private-dr-restore-drill" run that kicked off ~10 min ago. Can someone help triage? I’m oncall and trying to figure out if this is a real regression vs the usual staging flake.

Private-Installer Bot: Restore drill run failed
- env: staging
- cluster: priv-stg-usw2-02
- workflow: restore-drill-2025-01-28
- step: post-restore validation
- exit_code: 2
- logs: https://grafana.staging.redwood.ai/explore?orgId=1&left=%5B%22now-3h%22,%22now%22,%22loki%22,%7B%22expr%22:%22%7Bcluster%3D%5C%22priv-stg-usw2-02%5C%22,app%3D%5C%22private-installer%5C%22%7D%20%7C%3D%20%5C%22restore%20apply%5C%22%22%7D%5D
- artifacts: s3://redwood-private-staging-backups/restore-drills/2025-01-28/restore-drill-2025-01-28/ (kms key alias: alias/redwood-staging-backups)

Rafael Mendes: Is this the same failure mode as last week (missing CRDs / helm ordering)? Or something new?

Sean Gallagher: Not sure yet. Quick glance: looks like restore job completes, then validation complains about routing policies not present.

Sean Gallagher: Here’s the tail from the installer pod:
```text
INFO restore: applying backup manifest format=v1.2.0
INFO restore: restoring component=control-plane-db
INFO restore: restoring component=config
INFO restore: restoring component=helm-releases
WARN k8s: waiting for CRDs to be established…
INFO validate: checking tenant config + routing policies
ERROR validate: GET /control-plane/api/v1/routing/policies -> 404
ERROR validate: expected 6 policies, found 0
FATAL restore: post-restore validation failed
```

Sergio Costa: ^ 404 from control-plane API smells like control plane not ready (or hitting wrong svc) vs actual missing data. Do we have the trace/span for the validation request?

Sean Gallagher: Not yet. The explore link above should have spans but I didn’t dig.

Sergio Costa: Found it. Validation is calling `https://redwood-control-plane.redwood-private.svc:8443` (internal) and getting 404 from the router, not a connection error. That usually means the API server is up but route isn’t registered.

Bruno Silva: Route not registered could happen if the CRDs didn’t apply before the helm releases restore. We changed the helm hook ordering in pr-138 recently; did staging pick that up?

Sean Gallagher: Staging is on `private-installer 0.19.3` and charts `redwood-private 1.14.1` I think. Not sure if that includes pr-138.

Connor O'Brien: pr-138 landed in charts `1.14.2` (not in 1.14.1). If the restore is expecting the new hook, staging might be in an in-between state.

Benji Okafor: Another angle: does the backup include routing policies in this environment? We intentionally excluded some generated resources. But we should still restore the policy objects in the control-plane DB.

Michael Nguyen: If policies are DB-backed, 0 policies after restore suggests either (a) the DB restore didn’t happen / restored empty, or (b) we restored but control plane is pointing at a fresh DB.

Hanae Suzuki: Any KMS errors? If the DB snapshot decrypt failed, we might be falling back to "init new" silently (bad). Check for `envelope decrypt` logs.

Sean Gallagher: Good call. Grepping logs… I see:
```text
INFO crypto: using provider=kms key_alias=alias/redwood-staging-backups
INFO crypto: decrypted DEK ok
```
So probably not that.

Sergio Costa: In Loki I see something earlier:
```text
ERROR k8s: (Forbidden) cannot list resource "customresourcedefinitions" in API group "apiextensions.k8s.io" at the cluster scope
```
Looks like RBAC on the restore job SA.

Bruno Silva: Oh wow. That would explain CRDs not being present/verified, then helm restore proceeds and API routes never come up correctly.

Connor O'Brien: That matches INT-612-ish from a different thread. Did we fix RBAC in chart values or was it manual?

Bruno Silva: We fixed RBAC in helm chart pr-131 (backup cronjob RBAC) and pr-138 (restore job template). If staging is still on 1.14.1, it likely doesn’t have the CRD list permission for the restore Job.

Rafael Mendes: So likely not a product regression, but staging running old chart version vs restore drill expecting new behavior.

Sean Gallagher: What’s the right immediate action? Disable paging for this drill until we upgrade charts? Or hotfix RBAC in staging?

Benji Okafor: For oncall sanity: I’d hotfix RBAC in staging now so the drill signal is meaningful. Then file ticket to ensure drill uses pinned compatible versions.

Bruno Silva: I can patch the clusterrole + binding quickly. Minimal:
- allow `get,list,watch` on CRDs
- allow `get,list` on `validatingwebhookconfigurations` too (we’ve hit that before)
I’ll DM you the yaml.

Hanae Suzuki: +1 to patching. Also please confirm restore job SA name. Some clusters use `redwood-private-restore` vs `redwood-private-installer`.

Sean Gallagher: SA in logs is `redwood-private-restore`. If you have the exact manifests, I’ll apply.

Bruno Silva: Here you go (apply to `redwood-private` namespace). This is what’s in 1.14.2:
```yaml
apiVersion: rbac.authorization.k8s.io/v1
kind: ClusterRole
metadata:
  name: redwood-private-restore
rules:
- apiGroups: ["apiextensions.k8s.io"]
  resources: ["customresourcedefinitions"]
  verbs: ["get","list","watch"]
- apiGroups: ["admissionregistration.k8s.io"]
  resources: ["validatingwebhookconfigurations","mutatingwebhookconfigurations"]
  verbs: ["get","list","watch"]
---
apiVersion: rbac.authorization.k8s.io/v1
kind: ClusterRoleBinding
metadata:
  name: redwood-private-restore
roleRef:
  apiGroup: rbac.authorization.k8s.io
  kind: ClusterRole
  name: redwood-private-restore
subjects:
- kind: ServiceAccount
  name: redwood-private-restore
  namespace: redwood-private
```

Sean Gallagher: Applied. Re-running the workflow manually now.

Sergio Costa: While you rerun, I’ll add a note to the dashboard that drill failures should be ignored on 1.14.1. Also we should update the alert to include chart version label once we have it.

Rafael Mendes: Please capture this in the incident-style thread too so we don’t lose it. Action item: upgrade staging charts to >=1.14.2 and align installer expectations.

Michael Nguyen: One more thing: if CRD list was forbidden, we should ensure the restore script fails early (preflight) instead of limping into validation. Might be worth adding a guard.

Connor O'Brien: Agreed. I’ll open a follow-up in release checklist: staging must run same chart version as drill harness, otherwise we get noisy pages.

Sean Gallagher: Rerun result: restore/validation passed. :thumbsup:

Sean Gallagher: Summary for posterity: failure was RBAC on `redwood-private-restore` SA preventing CRD list/watch; led to missing route registration + validation 404s. Patched ClusterRole/Binding (same as charts 1.14.2) and rerun succeeded.

Rafael Mendes: Nice. Sean can you drop the Loki snippet + patch into the incidents thread too? I’ll link it to ENG-9827/9822 as a hardening item (fail fast on RBAC).

Sean Gallagher: Will do.

title: #eng-platform: 1737924411
preview:
Logan Wright: heads up: I think we have a gap for Console results dashboard after the attribution schema update. We added `{experiment_id, variant_id}` dims to the rollups, but existing rollup partitions (and some downstream cached datasets) won’t have those columns populated -> Console is going to show “no data” / flatlines for anything before deploy.

Mei Lin: yep, confirmed. The `route_variant_metrics_rollup` table is partitioned by hour and the new columns are NULL for pre-change partitions. The results API query groups by variant, so it drops the old partitions on the floor.

Chloe Martin: that explains what I’m seeing in staging — list/detail loads but charts only start at ~this morning. I assumed it was just low traffic :facepalm:

Caleb Johnson: +1. Ingestion is fine; it’s the aggregation layer. Raw spans have the attrs for ~N days, but the rollup job only started writing the new schema when we flipped it.

Dylan Brooks: do we need a backfill for beta/GA? If we only show data “since deploy” it’s going to be super confusing, esp for experiments that started earlier in the week.

Nadia Rahman: for cost/token deltas we also rely on the same rollups. If we don’t backfill, the cost panel will look like it’s missing historical baseline.

Allison Grant: from an observability standpoint, we should be explicit: what’s the retention for raw that we can backfill from? We can’t conjure attribution dims if they weren’t on the spans.

Caleb Johnson: good news: the dims *were* on the OTel spans starting ~Jan 20 (router context plumbing). The schema change here is basically “rollups now include the dims”. So we can recompute rollups from raw spans for the overlap window.

Mei Lin: constraints:
- raw trace/span retention in the analytics bucket is 14 days (rolling)
- long-term metrics store has 30 days, but it didn’t include the new tags until very recently (and we intentionally sampled some high-cardinality tags)
So practical backfill is “last 10-14 days” max.

Logan Wright: ok, so proposal:
1) run a one-off backfill job to rebuild rollup partitions for the last 14 days from the raw span export (where `experiment_id/variant_id` exist)
2) rerun downstream materialized views / cached datasets used by results API
3) add a banner in Console: “Experiment metrics available from <timestamp>” if older than backfill window

Rafael Mendes: +1 and we should add an alert for “results dashboard empty while traffic > 0” to catch this earlier next time.

Jada Williams: careful: reprocessing old partitions has to be idempotent + won’t double count. Recommend writing to `*_v2` temp table and swap, or do partition overwrite with explicit `run_id` guard.

Mei Lin: I can do partition overwrite in the warehouse safely as long as we lock to a bounded time range and disable the incremental job during overwrite.

Dylan Brooks: what’s the cost/ETA?

Logan Wright: rough math from yesterday’s span volumes:
- ~270M request-level span rows over 14d across prod (post-sampling), plus token spans are way bigger but rollup only needs request spans + cost records.
- scan size ~6.5–8.0 TB depending on predicate pushdown
- warehouse estimate: ~$650–$900 in compute + storage churn (assuming we do it in 6-hour chunks to avoid long queries)
- runtime: ~4–6 hours wall-clock if we parallelize by day

Caleb Johnson: that’s acceptable for beta. We should also throttle to keep from stepping on other ETLs (we already had query cost spikes when results queries got expensive).

Chloe Martin: for Console, if backfill completes today we can remove a bunch of “why is it blank” support churn. Support already pinged me about the charts.

Connor O'Brien: if we’re doing this, I need it on the release checklist. Also we should document that schema changes to attribution dims require backfill as a standard step.

Allison Grant: also: make sure the backfill doesn’t reintroduce high-cardinality tags into metrics streams we purposely restricted. Keep it to the canonical dims only: `{route_id, experiment_id, variant_id, model_id, region}`.

- Mei: draft the backfill SQL + runbook steps (partition overwrite + disable/enable incremental rollup job)
- Logan: sanity-check cost estimate + get quick :eyes: from SRE re: warehouse load window
- Caleb: confirm raw span export has stable field names + no missing attrs for that window
- Chloe: add temporary UI note “metrics available since…” + handle empty state copy
- Connor: add checklist item + coordinate timing with release train

Logan Wright: sounds good. I’ll file this as ENG-9034 if it isn’t already, and link in here.

Rafael Mendes: please include a rollback: if backfill starts causing warehouse pain, stop after N days and at least backfill the last 48h.

Caleb Johnson: one more gotcha: attribution drop rate was non-trivial on streaming retries earlier this week. Backfill won’t fix missing attribution; it’ll just recompute what exists. So results might still show some “unattributed” bucket unless we filter it.

Jada Williams: yeah, for results UI we should either (a) exclude unattributed from variant comparison and show a small “X% unattributed” indicator, or (b) treat it as a data quality guardrail.

Chloe Martin: I’ll add a small callout. We already have guardrail banners; can reuse pattern.

Logan Wright: ok. target: start backfill tonight 7pm PT off-peak. I’ll post when the job is queued.

—

Connor O'Brien: (next morning) did it run?

Mei Lin: kicked off at 19:12 PT, finished 00:48 PT. Overwrote 14 days of partitions. Total scan 7.4TB. Compute cost came out to ~$812.

Caleb Johnson: spot checked attribution completeness: last 7d looks good; before Jan 20 there’s still basically nothing (as expected).

Chloe Martin: Console charts now show data back to Jan 20 for my test experiment. Empty-state copy updated for earlier windows. :thumbsup:

Logan Wright: great. I’ll add a follow-up to formalize “schema change -> backfill plan” in the metrics taxonomy doc + release checklist.

title: #eng-sre: 1737839022
preview:
Sean Gallagher: Heads up: proposing a game day next week to simulate *experiment config propagation lag* and verify alerts + runbooks + rollback steps before we broaden beta. Goal is to reproduce the staging “stale config” behavior on demand and make sure on-call muscle memory is there.

Sean Gallagher: Suggested window: Thu 10:00–11:30 PT. @Rafael Mendes can you run the facilitation?

Rafael Mendes: yup. I can drive. Let’s align on the exact scenario(s) so it’s not just “break stuff and vibe” :)

Allison Grant: +1. We should also validate the Grafana panels + alert routing. Last time we had propagation-lag we only noticed because Console results looked stale.

Aisha Bello: If we do this, I want clear “stop conditions” + a safety plan. We can do it in staging only, but we’ve had staging metrics changes spill over into shared alert channels.

Connor O'Brien: Fwiw release train freeze starts Fri noon. If this uncovers changes, we need a quick decision on whether it blocks.

Ethan Park: From control plane side: we can simulate config propagation lag by forcing the policy engine cache TTL to something huge or by blocking the config watcher stream. There’s a debug knob in staging.

Jordan Lee: For data plane: easiest is to drop/slow the config refresh from experiment-service -> router evaluator. In the evaluator we also have a “treat config missing as disabled” default; we should verify we get the behavior we want when config is stale vs unavailable.

Rafael Mendes: Ok, proposal for the game day plan:
1) Baseline: start a small route experiment on a test route in staging (50/50) and confirm normal attribution + split.
2) Inject lag: block config propagation (simulate 20+ min staleness) while making a config change (e.g. ramp from 50/50 to 90/10).
3) Verify detection: propagation lag metric + alert fires; Console shows banner/guardrail; runbook steps are accurate.
4) Verify mitigation: rollback the change + optionally stop experiment; confirm traffic returns to expected.

Chloe Martin: For Console, we have the “config staleness” banner behind the route experiments flag. It shows when results API sees config_version mismatch > threshold. We should verify the copy + that it links to the runbook.

Allison Grant: Re dashboards/alerts, the ones we care about:
- `experiment_config_propagation_lag_seconds` (p95)
- `experiment_config_staleness_seconds` (max)
- attribution drop rate panel (just to ensure we don’t confuse those)

Logan Wright: Please also keep an eye on telemetry. If propagation is lagged, attribution might be correct but *expected/observed* variant ratio changes will lag. We need to make sure people don’t mis-diagnose it as split skew.

Aisha Bello: Can we define “success” for the game day? Like: alert fires within X minutes of injection; the page includes runbook link; mitigation returns lag metric to normal within Y.

Rafael Mendes: +1. Draft success criteria:
- Alert “Experiment config propagation lag” fires within 5 min of crossing threshold.
- Alert payload includes runbook URL + dashboard link.
- On-call can follow runbook to identify stale config version + verify it’s not just results aggregation lag.
- Mitigation (unblock watcher / restart cache) reduces lag below threshold within 10 min.
- We can confirm the routing decision explains stale version via debug endpoint.

Sean Gallagher: Perfect. Also want to check escalation path: SRE -> Control Plane -> Policy Engine, with clear ownership.

Ethan Park: Ownership wise: propagation pipeline is shared (experiment-service publishes, policy engine consumes). If the watcher stream is blocked, that’s on us (control plane). If evaluator caching is sticky beyond staleness bounds, that’s on policy engine.

Jordan Lee: We also need to confirm the fallback behavior. If config is stale, we should keep using last-known-good; if config is unavailable, we should fail “safe” (no experiment) but still route via normal rollout/fallback policy.

Chloe Martin: Question: do we want to test the “stop experiment” semantics during staleness? i.e., user hits Stop in Console but the router still applies old config for N minutes.

Rafael Mendes: Yes, that’s a great second scenario, but keep it bounded. Maybe do it as step 2b if time permits.

Allison Grant: Also can we ensure the alert doesn’t page *all* of #eng-sre? We should route it to staging-only / low severity for the game day.

Aisha Bello: +1 please. The last attribution-drop alert was noisy and people ignored it after.

Sean Gallagher: Agree. For the game day we’ll temporarily route alerts to `#eng-sre` only (no paging), label as “GAMEDAY”, then revert.

Incident Bot: Reminder: Any alert routing changes must be reverted post-event. Owner: Rafael Mendes. Checklist: update rule -> post change -> validate -> revert.

Rafael Mendes: Noted.

Rafael Mendes: Prep checklist (by EOD Wed):
- [@Ethan Park] confirm staging knob to block watcher stream + how to unblock
- [@Jordan Lee] confirm evaluator cache behavior + safe restart steps
- [@Allison Grant] provide direct Grafana links + confirm alert rule threshold
- [@Chloe Martin] confirm Console banner is enabled in staging + where it sources staleness
- [@Logan Wright] confirm where to query config_version / attribution fields

Ethan Park: For blocking: we can toggle `EXPERIMENT_CONFIG_WATCHER_PAUSE=true` on the policy-engine staging deployment. It pauses consuming updates but keeps serving.

Jordan Lee: If we do that, we should also note what “staleness” means: we track `config_last_refresh_ts` per instance. Alert uses p95 across instances. We should pick a threshold that guarantees it triggers even if one instance is refreshing.

Allison Grant: The current draft alert is p95 > 300s for 10m. For game day we can temporarily set to 120s for 5m, just in staging.

Aisha Bello: Please document the temporary change in-thread and in the runbook so nobody thinks we had an actual incident.

Rafael Mendes: Will do.

Chloe Martin: Console banner: route experiments -> details page -> “Config may be stale” banner. It triggers when results API sees `router_config_version != control_plane_version` AND last_seen_update_age > 5m.

Logan Wright: For attribution / version checks:
- OTel span attrs: `experiment.id`, `experiment.variant`, `experiment.config_version`
- Router logs: `exp_cfg_ver` and `cfg_age_s`
We can query via Honeycomb: `service=router-staging` + group by `exp_cfg_ver`.

Sean Gallagher: Great. During game day, we should record timings in-thread: injection time, alert fire time, mitigation time, recovery time.

Rafael Mendes: Agenda for Thu 10:00 PT:
- 10:00 kickoff + roles (facilitator, observer, scribe)
- 10:10 baseline validate
- 10:20 inject lag + change traffic split
- 10:25 observe metrics + wait for alert
- 10:40 follow runbook + debug endpoint
- 11:00 mitigation + confirm recovery
- 11:15 retro + action items

Connor O'Brien: Works. Please add me to invite; I’ll track any “release blocking” follow-ups.

Allison Grant: I’ll drop dashboard links here before then.

Ethan Park: Confirmed on the knob + revert steps. We should also set a hard stop: if anything looks like it might impact non-staging, we abort and roll back immediately.

Aisha Bello: +1. Also: do not touch shared prod alert routes. Only staging.

Rafael Mendes: ack.

Rafael Mendes: One more: should we test auto-stop? If propagation lag causes guardrail evaluation to misfire, we don’t want auto-stop thrashing.

Sean Gallagher: Let’s not test hard-stop during this game day. Soft-stop/notification only. We can do a separate game day for guardrail false positives.

Jordan Lee: +1. Mixing scenarios will make it hard to interpret.

Allison Grant: agreed.

Rafael Mendes: Cool. I’ll send calendar + keep this thread as the scratchpad. Please add any gotchas / links.

Incident Bot: Calendar event created: “GAMEDAY: Experiment config propagation lag (staging)” (Thu 10:00–11:30 PT). Organizer: Rafael Mendes. Attendees: Sean Gallagher, Allison Grant, Aisha Bello, Ethan Park, Jordan Lee, Chloe Martin, Logan Wright, Connor O’Brien.

Allison Grant: Dashboards:
- Grafana: https://grafana.redwood.dev/d/exp-prop-lag/experiment-config-propagation?var=env=staging
- Alert rule (staging): https://grafana.redwood.dev/alerting/rules/exp-config-prop-lag-staging
- Runbook draft: https://confluence.redwood.dev/display/SRE/Experiment+config+propagation+lag

Rafael Mendes: Nice. I’ll update runbook to include the game day steps + the “temporary threshold change” note.

Sean Gallagher: Thanks all. Let’s make sure we walk away with 1) validated detection 2) validated mitigation 3) clear ownership in the runbook.

Rafael Mendes: :thumbsup: I’ll post final checklist Wed afternoon.

title: #eng-platform: 1737750121
preview:
Logan Wright: Hey folks — need quick alignment on OTel span attribute names for route experiments before we lock PR-28489. We already tag `rw.route.id`, `rw.model.id`, etc. What should we use for experiment + variant?

Caleb Johnson: +1. Current draft in the PR uses `experiment_id` / `variant_id` (flat) which doesn’t match anything else we do. Also worried about collisions with generic “experiment” usage.

Allison Grant: We should follow our existing convention: lowercase, vendor prefix, dot-separated. OTel spec doesn’t require dots, but it’s what we’ve been doing for custom attrs (`rw.*`).

Jordan Lee: On the routing side we have fields named `experiment_id` and `variant_id` in the request context. Happy to map to whatever attribute keys we standardize.

Jada Williams: Also: please don’t add both “route_experiment” and “canary” attributes with different naming styles. Console is going to pivot on these.

Rafael Mendes: SRE angle: keep it consistent so it’s searchable in traces. Also, if we can avoid adding attributes on every child span that’d help. We only need them on the top-level request span + maybe inference span.

Chloe Martin: For the Console results API we’re joining on `{route_id, experiment_id, variant_id}`. If we pick names now, we should mirror those dimensions in telemetry.

Logan Wright: Proposed options (based on what’s in-flight):
1) `rw.experiment_id`, `rw.variant_id`
2) `rw.experiment.id`, `rw.experiment.variant_id`
3) `rw.route.experiment.id`, `rw.route.experiment.variant`

Caleb Johnson: I’d vote #2. Dot structure reads better, and we can later add `rw.experiment.allocation` or `rw.experiment.bucket` if needed without inventing new roots.

Allison Grant: Agree #2. Also keep `variant_id` vs `variant`? We’ve used `*.id` for IDs consistently.

Jordan Lee: Variant is an ID string anyway (like `control` / `v2-quant`), but being explicit doesn’t hurt.

Rafael Mendes: Whatever we pick, please document “omit when not in experiment” (don’t send empty string). Makes filters + cardinality less gross.

Logan Wright: Yep.

Jada Williams: One more: should it be `rw.experiment.variant.id` vs `rw.experiment.variant_id`? If we’re going dots, might as well be fully nested.

Allison Grant: I’d avoid going too deep for now. We don’t have any other `rw.foo.bar.baz` keys today besides a couple in auth. But I’m OK either way.

Caleb Johnson: Depth isn’t a big deal but let’s not bike-shed. I slightly prefer:
- `rw.experiment.id`
- `rw.experiment.variant_id`

Jordan Lee: Why not `rw.experiment.variant.id`?

Caleb Johnson: Because we’ll end up with 3 spellings across services. In practice folks type these in Grafana/Tempo search; shorter is better.

Rafael Mendes: +1 shorter. We can always add `rw.experiment.variant_name` later if we really need it.

Chloe Martin: Console is going to display “Variant” and store `variant_id`. I’d keep the attribute key matching the storage field name (`variant_id`).

title: #eng: 1737581002
preview:
Amina Yusuf: Working on private-installer audit events (upgrade/rollback/config apply). Main open question: how do we represent operator identity in Private when someone runs `redwood-private install/upgrade` from a bastion / laptop? We can’t always rely on OIDC.

Diego Santos: yeah this is the parity gap vs Hosted/Dedicated. Hosted we have `actor.user_id` + auth context + source_ip. In Private, “operator” might be (a) human running CLI, (b) automation (Ansible, Argo, etc). We should model both.

Vivek Kulkarni: +1. For enterprise customers, they keep asking “who did the upgrade” and “who changed exporter settings”. Even if it’s “unknown automation” we need something consistent.

Kevin Osei: Reminder from schema discussions: we need stable semantics, minimal PII, and good dedupe. For Private installer events, propose:
- `actor.type`: `human` | `service` | `unknown`
- `actor.id`: if known (OIDC sub, customer SSO GUID, or service principal)
- `actor.display_name`: optional, but would be high-cardinality + often PII. We should avoid unless we’re hashing.
- `actor.email_sha256`: optional if we have email, but only if customer opts in? (also doesn’t help if no email)

Hanae Suzuki: +1 avoid raw email. We already have redaction rules draft that treats email as PII. Hash is OK but still sensitive-ish; needs consistent salt story (can’t be Redwood secret in air-gapped env).

Amina Yusuf: Here’s what the installer currently can reliably collect:
- CLI has `--operator-id` (we can add) + `--operator-type` (human/service)
- In interactive mode, we can optionally prompt `operator` (freeform) but that’s the worst for cardinality
- In k8s, we can read `KUBECONFIG` context user name (but that’s messy / sometimes includes email)
- We always have `installer_instance_id` and `installer_version`

Rafael Mendes: From incident forensics POV, even “unknown” is better than nothing as long as we have correlation IDs + the host it ran on. Can we capture `execution_environment` like `host.hostname` or `node`? (but careful: could leak internal naming)

Sean Gallagher: SRE take: do NOT block upgrade/rollback on identity collection. During an outage we need to run rollback fast. Identity should be best-effort.

Diego Santos: Agree. We can treat actor fields as best-effort but the event itself must be emitted. We should add `actor.attribution`: `explicit` (flag provided) vs `inferred` (kube context) vs `unknown`.

Kevin Osei: That helps for customer guidance too: “If you want strong operator attribution, pass operator-id via automation or configure OIDC.”

Connor O'Brien: Release perspective: we should avoid adding an interactive prompt by default. Flags/env vars are safer for automation.

Amina Yusuf: Proposal v1 (please poke holes):
- Add flags/env:
  - `--actor-type` / `RW_AUDIT_ACTOR_TYPE` in {human, service}
  - `--actor-id` / `RW_AUDIT_ACTOR_ID` (string, recommended stable GUID)
  - `--actor-idp` / `RW_AUDIT_ACTOR_IDP` (optional, e.g. okta, aad, local)
- Installer emits audit events with:
  - `actor.type` from flag else `unknown`
  - `actor.id` from flag else empty
  - `actor.attribution` = explicit|unknown
  - `client.name` = `redwood-private-installer`, `client.version` = installer version
  - `source.ip` omitted (or null) in Private
- Add `operator` info as a namespaced extension field if we need more later (e.g. `private.operator.*`)

Hanae Suzuki: looks good. One tweak: if we include `actor.idp`, limit allowed values (enum) to avoid random strings -> cardinality.

Diego Santos: +1. Also for parity: use the same top-level `actor.*` fields as schema v1, don’t invent `operator.*` at top-level. Put anything installer-specific under `private_installer.*` extension.

Leila Farouk: For E2E tests, we can set `RW_AUDIT_ACTOR_TYPE=service` and `RW_AUDIT_ACTOR_ID=ci-fixture` and assert it shows up in the event payload. That’ll keep coverage tests deterministic.

Rafael Mendes: For rollback, make sure we emit BOTH attempt + result events (start/success/failure) so we can see failed rollbacks too. Operator identity should be copied across them.

Kevin Osei: and add `event_id` (ULID) + `admin_action_id` for the whole run so SIEM can group. Even if retries happen, de-dupe by `event_id`.

Amina Yusuf: Cool. I’ll implement:
- `admin_action_id` generated once per CLI invocation
- event types:
  - `private.installer.upgrade.started|completed|failed`
  - `private.installer.rollback.started|completed|failed`
  - `private.installer.config_apply.started|completed|failed`
- actor fields best-effort via flags/env; no kube-context sniffing in v1

DeployBot: DeployBot APP  [automated] queued checks for redwood-private-installer/pr-611-emit-audit-events-during-install-upgrade-rollback

Sean Gallagher: please add a short note in the runbook/docs: “If actor is unknown, look for bastion host logs / change ticket refs using admin_action_id”. Customers will ask.

Vivek Kulkarni: +1. And we should align wording with Security FAQ: “Private installer supports operator attribution via provided identifiers; otherwise events will show actor as unknown.” No promises beyond that.

Amina Yusuf: ack. I’ll drop a comment on PR with the proposed fields + ask Kevin/Hanae for schema sanity review.

title: #eng-oncall: 1737506650
preview:
Ivy Chen: oncall q: how do we *validate audit log delivery* during a partial region outage? like us-east degraded but us-west ok. i want something more concrete than “check dashboards are green” before we tell Support/customers. what’s the current playbook?

Datadog: [ALERT] audit-ingest - delivery_latency_p95 breached (region=us-east-1, env=prod) | p95=92s threshold=60s

Sean Gallagher: good q. we don’t have a single “push button” validation yet, but there are a couple checks that work together.

Sean Gallagher: first: confirm whether we’re in “ingest backlog” vs “producer not emitting” vs “exporter blocked”. region outages can look like all 3 depending on which component is impacted.

Ivy Chen: yeah. specifically: if us-east is partially down, requests may still be served from us-west. how do we prove the audit events for those actions still make it to the sink + exports?

Rafael Mendes: runbook draft covers this, but it’s a bit buried. linking: https://confluence.redwoodinference.com/display/ENGSRE/Audit+Log+Pipeline+Incident+Runbook (section “Regional impairment / failover validation”).

Kevin Osei: from exporter side: delivery is at-least-once. during region flaps you might see duplicates. validation should focus on: (1) events still arriving (2) no sustained drops (3) latency trending back down once region recovers.

Allison Grant: +1. also make sure you’re looking at the right graphs. audit pipeline is split:
- producer emit (service logs / counters)
- ingest accepted (gateway -> audit ingest)
- queue/buffer depth
- exporter success/error per target

Logan Wright: we’ve got the “expected vs observed” view in the coverage dashboard but it’s not perfect for real-time outage validation. still useful to sanity check that the sensitive event types are continuing to show up.

Ivy Chen: can someone give a concrete step list? like: “perform action X in region Y” and then “search for event_id Z”

Amara Diallo: for hosted/dedicated you can do a safe synthetic: hit the admin test endpoint? we don’t have a dedicated “audit test event” endpoint yet.

Sean Gallagher: we intentionally avoided a public “emit arbitrary audit event” endpoint. but we can do a low-risk sensitive action in a sandbox tenant.

Benji Okafor: easiest RBAC action in a non-prod tenant: create a temp role and delete it. that should emit:
- rbac.role.create
- rbac.role.delete
and it exercises actor + target + diff fields.

Nadia Rahman: if you’re validating *region failover*, make sure the request actually routes to the alternate region. in prod, you can force region via header in internal tooling, or do it from a host that prefers us-west. otherwise you may think you’re testing us-west but still hitting us-east.

Jordan Lee: for policy changes (also sensitive) there’s usually more guardrails. RBAC change in an internal test tenant is better.

Ivy Chen: ok, so:
1) do RBAC create/delete in sandbox tenant
2) confirm event shows up
but where do i look to confirm it “made it” end-to-end?

Kevin Osei: if you have access to the raw audit stream (internal), quickest is querying ingest store by correlation IDs.
- grab request_id / trace_id from the API response headers or service logs
- search audit-events index for request_id

Kevin Osei: if you’re validating SIEM export specifically, pick one configured internal Splunk HEC target (we have the staging one) and check the exporter “delivered_events_total{target=splunk_hec}” increments + sample payload includes event_id.

Rafael Mendes: for oncall, we usually do:
A) dashboards: https://grafana.redwoodinference.com/d/audit-pipeline/audit-log-pipeline (latency, drop rate, queue depth by region)
B) spot check: search audit event store for the synthetic action by request_id
C) exporter check: exporter success rate + if customer is impacted, have Support ask for a sample of received events and verify event_id continuity.

Allison Grant: and for partial region outage, the key is comparing producer emits vs ingest accepts.
- if producer emits keep increasing but ingest accepts flatlines in us-east -> ingest path issue
- if both flatline -> requests may not be hitting that region or producers failing

Sean Gallagher: concrete “do this now” (draft, until we formalize):
1. Pick internal sandbox tenant ("sec-audit-sandbox").
2. From a host that routes to us-west (or set internal routing override), call control-plane RBAC create role. Immediately delete role.
3. Capture request_id from response headers.
4. Query audit-events index: `request_id:<id>` and confirm you see the two events with `schema_version=v1`.
5. Confirm `ingest_accepted_total` increased in us-west and `drop_rate` not elevated.
6. If validating SIEM export: confirm exporter `delivered_events_total` increases and the payload contains the same `event_id` values. Remind: duplicates possible; dedupe by event_id.

Connor O'Brien: re: routing override, internal doc: https://docs.redwoodinference.com/internal/routing/region-override (works for control-plane + console). might be stale though.

Ivy Chen: is there a “known good” way to query audit-events index? i don’t have the exact kibana/grafana panel handy.

Logan Wright: use the “Audit Events Search” saved view in OpenSearch Dashboards: `prod-audit-events-*`. filter fields:
- `tenant_id` = sec-audit-sandbox
- `event_type` = rbac.role.create OR rbac.role.delete
- `request_id` = ...

Logan Wright: if you don’t see it there but producer counters increment, it’s an ingest problem. if you see it there but customer export is missing, it’s exporter/target.

Rafael Mendes: we should add this as a small explicit subsection in the runbook. I’ll update the draft with these steps + links to the saved view.

Sean Gallagher: +1. Ivy, in the meantime if you’re mid-incident: paste the request_id here and we can help confirm in the index.

Ivy Chen: cool. i’ll run the RBAC create/delete with west routing override and report back.

Ivy Chen: update: ran create/delete, got request_id `req_9b7c1a...`.

Kevin Osei: found both events in `prod-audit-events-*` with that request_id. `event_id` present, `schema_version=v1`, `region=us-west-2`, `delivery_status=accepted`.

Allison Grant: grafana shows ingest latency p95 back down to 18s in us-east now; queue depth draining. i’d call out “no drops observed, temporary latency increase” in incident notes.

Sean Gallagher: nice. for customer comms: be careful to not promise “exactly once”. we can say “at-least-once delivery; dedupe using event_id” if they ask.

Rafael Mendes: action item: I’ll PR the runbook change + add a short “partial region outage validation checklist” with these exact steps + links.

Ivy Chen: thanks all. this is what i needed.

1) do RBAC create/delete in sandbox tenant
2) confirm event shows up
but where do i look to confirm it “made it” end-to-end?

Kevin Osei: if you have access to the raw audit stream (internal), quickest is querying ingest store by correlation IDs.
- grab request_id / trace_id from the API response headers or service logs
- search audit-events index for request_id

Kevin Osei: if you’re validating SIEM export specifically, pick one configured internal Splunk HEC target (we have the staging one) and check the exporter “delivered_events_total{target=splunk_hec}” increments + sample payload includes event_id.

Rafael Mendes: for oncall, we usually do:
A) dashboards: https://grafana.redwoodinference.com/d/audit-pipeline/audit-log-pipeline (latency, drop rate, queue depth by region)
B) spot check: search audit event store for the synthetic action by request_id
C) exporter check: exporter success rate + if customer is impacted, have Support ask for a sample of received events and verify event_id continuity.

Allison Grant: and for partial region outage, the key is comparing producer emits vs ingest accepts.
- if producer emits keep increasing but ingest accepts flatlines in us-east -> ingest path issue
- if both flatline -> requests may not be hitting that region or producers failing

Sean Gallagher: concrete “do this now” (draft, until we formalize):
1. Pick internal sandbox tenant ("sec-audit-sandbox").
2. From a host that routes to us-west (or set internal routing override), call control-plane RBAC create role. Immediately delete role.
3. Capture request_id from response headers.
4. Query audit-events index: `request_id:<id>` and confirm you see the two events with `schema_version=v1`.
5. Confirm `ingest_accepted_total` increased in us-west and `drop_rate` not elevated.
6. If validating SIEM export: confirm exporter `delivered_events_total` increases and the payload contains the same `event_id` values. Remind: duplicates possible; dedupe by event_id.

Connor O'Brien: re: routing override, internal doc: https://docs.redwoodinference.com/internal/routing/region-override (works for control-plane + console). might be stale though.

Ivy Chen: is there a “known good” way to query audit-events index? i don’t have the exact kibana/grafana panel handy.

Logan Wright: use the “Audit Events Search” saved view in OpenSearch Dashboards: `prod-audit-events-*`. filter fields:
- `tenant_id` = sec-audit-sandbox
- `event_type` = rbac.role.create OR rbac.role.delete
- `request_id` = ...

Logan Wright: if you don’t see it there but producer counters increment, it’s an ingest problem. if you see it there but customer export is missing, it’s exporter/target.

Rafael Mendes: we should add this as a small explicit subsection in the runbook. I’ll update the draft with these steps + links to the saved view.

Sean Gallagher: +1. Ivy, in the meantime if you’re mid-incident: paste the request_id here and we can help confirm in the index.

Ivy Chen: cool. i’ll run the RBAC create/delete with west routing override and report back.

Ivy Chen: update: ran create/delete, got request_id `req_9b7c1a...`.

Kevin Osei: found both events in `prod-audit-events-*` with that request_id. `event_id` present, `schema_version=v1`, `region=us-west-2`, `delivery_status=accepted`.

Allison Grant: grafana shows ingest latency p95 back down to 18s in us-east now; queue depth draining. i’d call out “no drops observed, temporary latency increase” in incident notes.

Sean Gallagher: nice. for customer comms: be careful to not promise “exactly once”. we can say “at-least-once delivery; dedupe using event_id” if they ask.

Rafael Mendes: action item: I’ll PR the runbook change + add a short “partial region outage validation checklist” with these exact steps + links.

Ivy Chen: thanks all. this is what i needed.

title: #eng: 1737228441
preview:
Sahana Iyer: trying to lock down an integration test plan for split accuracy (route experiments) before we ask for any kind of load test. what's the best way to validate allocation % + determinism without spinning up a giant traffic generator?

Leila Farouk: +1. e2e tests today can prove “request gets variant header” but not “50/50 is actually 50/50”. we also want something that runs in CI and doesn’t flap.

Jordan Lee: we can get most of the confidence with simulation (no GPUs, no real traffic). assignment is deterministic hash -> bucket -> variant. so test the assigner as a pure function.

Jordan Lee: proposal:
1) generate N synthetic stickiness keys (uuid4 / random 128-bit) with a fixed seed.
2) run `assign(experiment_config, key)` for each key.
3) count variant hits, assert within tolerance for expected split.
4) repeat for a few configs (50/50, 90/10, 34/33/33, tiny buckets) and for “missing key” paths.

Jordan Lee: importantly: treat it as a statistical test w/ deterministic input. don’t rely on true randomness.

Leila Farouk: what N are we thinking? and what tolerance so it’s not flaky?

Jordan Lee: N=100k is cheap in unit/integration land (just hashing). if we don’t want that big, N=20k might be ok. tolerance can be based on binomial stddev.

Jordan Lee: e.g. expected p=0.5, N=20k => sigma ~ sqrt(N*p*(1-p)) ~ 70.7. 5 sigma is 353, so assert counts in [9650, 10350] (±1.75%). that’s extremely unlikely to fail given fixed seed anyway.

Jada Williams: +1 on simulation. we should also test “precedence + determinism across restarts” (same config version, same key => same variant).

Jada Williams: another thing: for multi-variant, use chi-square instead of per-variant tolerance. but per-variant bounds are easier to understand.

Vikram Singh: this covers the bucketing lib, but we also need router integration: ensure we’re not accidentally re-hashing different keys depending on headers present.

Vikram Singh: can do a lightweight integration test against router/policy engine in-process:
- stand up policy evaluator w/ an experiment config
- feed request contexts with different combinations of stickiness inputs (customer_id, user_id header, session_id, fallback)
- ensure key precedence matches ADR and assigned variant matches the pure assigner for the resolved key

Logan Wright: + reminder: if folks try to use metrics to validate split accuracy, it’s noisy in staging due to sampling + attribution drop. for correctness tests, prefer the pure assignment + router debug endpoint output.

Caleb Johnson: +1. telemetry is great for prod monitoring, not for CI validation of distribution.

Leila Farouk: can we put this under “integration tests” even if it’s mostly simulation? i’m thinking we keep it close to ENG-9031 scope.

Jordan Lee: yep. we can call it “distribution simulation test” but run it in the same suite.

Sahana Iyer: does anyone have a suggested structure for the test so it doesn’t take forever in CI?

Jordan Lee: rough pseudocode:

```python
import random
from collections import Counter

SEED = 1337
N = 20000

configs = [
  ("50-50", {"A": 0.5, "B": 0.5}),
  ("90-10", {"A": 0.9, "B": 0.1}),
  ("3-way", {"A": 0.34, "B": 0.33, "C": 0.33}),
]

def gen_keys(n):
  r = random.Random(SEED)
  return [f"user-{r.getrandbits(128):032x}" for _ in range(n)]

keys = gen_keys(N)

for name, split in configs:
  counts = Counter(assign(split, k) for k in keys)
  for v, p in split.items():
    # 5-sigma bound + small epsilon
    mu = N * p
    sigma = (N * p * (1 - p)) ** 0.5
    assert abs(counts[v] - mu) <= max(5*sigma, 0.005*N)
```

Jordan Lee: since keys are deterministic, it’s not flaky. runtime is basically N hash ops per config.

Rafael Mendes: can we also add a skew regression test for “missing stickiness key” defaulting? we had that bug where it used low-entropy / same value => variant 0 traffic.

Jordan Lee: yes. for missing key behavior we should test:
- if stickiness key missing and we fall back to IP hash, distribution should still look ok across synthetic IPs
- if we fall back to a constant or too-low entropy value, it’ll show up as >99% in one bucket. we can assert that “resolved key must not be constant” in validation, but also include a test that simulates “missing key” path produces non-degenerate distribution when given varying fallbacks.

Vikram Singh: router-side we can call the internal assignment debug endpoint (in test mode) and validate:
- it reports which key source it used (customer_id vs header vs session)
- it reports config version
- it returns variant_id
then compare that variant_id to the pure assigner output.

Leila Farouk: that’s nice because it tests the “resolved key” logic without spinning up full tracing/metrics.

Sahana Iyer: ok, so plan is:
1) simulation distribution test for bucketer
2) in-process router/policy evaluator test that compares debug output vs pure assigner
3) a tiny smoke e2e just to ensure headers propagate (variant header present) (already have)

Jada Williams: yep. and maybe one more: “config change doesn’t remap users unless split boundaries change” (sticky). e.g. if you rename a variant but keep same allocation, are we stable? depends on variant_id hashing input.

Jordan Lee: good call. in ADR we said hashing uses stable `variant_id` ordering + cumulative split. so if variant IDs change, assignment changes. we should make that explicit in tests + docs. for tests, assert that reordering variants in config does NOT change mapping (we normalize by variant_id sort) but changing variant_id does.

Caleb Johnson: please also keep N modest. 20k * 3 configs is fine. 100k might be ok but python overhead could be higher in CI.

Sahana Iyer: got it. i’ll start with N=20k, 3 configs, 5-sigma bound + 0.5% floor.

Redwood CI Bot: buildkite: started job `eng/integration-tests (route-experiments)` on branch `sahana/split-sim-tests`

Leila Farouk: can you add a note in the test comment about why we use sigma bounds so future folks don’t “tighten” it and cause pain?

Sahana Iyer: yep.

Rafael Mendes: also add a quick assertion for “no variant gets <1% traffic when configured >10%” (catches totally broken paths). sigma bounds should already catch it but the explicit guard reads well.

Jordan Lee: +1. and we should pin the seed so any failure is reproducible locally.

Sahana Iyer: cool. i’ll write up the test plan in ENG-9031 and link this thread. thanks all.

Leila Farouk: :thumbsup:

Vikram Singh: :+1: ping me when you have the debug-endpoint compare test, i want to make sure it matches router behavior.

Logan Wright: and if anyone wants to cross-check in staging later, use the experiment health dashboard, but don’t block CI on telemetry numbers.

Sahana Iyer: acknowledged.

title: #eng-platform: 1736879041
preview:
Jada Williams: quick review request: request-context plumbing for route experiments → tracing/metrics. want to sanity check we’re propagating `{experiment_id, variant_id, config_version}` consistently across router -> runtime -> otel spans -> metrics emitters. code pointers:
- `redwood/router/request_context.go` (new fields on `RequestContext`)
- `redwood/policy/experiment_evaluator.go` (assignment)
- `redwood/observability/otel/span_tags.go` (tagging)
PRs: `pr-28470` + `pr-28489`. anything we’re missing before we cut beta?

Jordan Lee: looked this morning. overall shape good. 2 things:
1) please make sure we set assignment *before* fallback policy eval (otherwise we’ll sometimes tag spans with the fallback model’s “effective” selection but no experiment fields). i think `pr-28457` changed order but i want to confirm `RequestContext` gets populated in the same block.
2) determinism: assignment should only depend on normalized route_id + stickiness key + experiment_id + config_version. don’t include model_id in hash input.

Elena Popov: +1. current flow in `route_selection.go` is:
```go
// (1) resolve route
// (2) evaluate experiment
// (3) choose variant -> set ctx.Experiment*
// (4) apply canary/fallback policies
```
so i think order is ok.

Logan Wright: on the telemetry side: we want to avoid “half attribution” where otel spans have experiment tags but our metrics don’t (or vice versa). what’s the canonical source for the experiment fields? `RequestContext` seems fine, just ensure both tracing + metrics read from it.

Caleb Johnson: question on naming. in `span_tags.go` i saw:
- `redwood.experiment_id`
- `redwood.experiment_variant_id`
- `redwood.route_id`
We also have older conventions using `routing.*` tags. any preference?

Allison Grant: please keep it aligned with existing OTel conventions doc: prefix internal fields with `redwood.` is fine. don’t introduce `experiment.*` without a namespace (we’ll collide w vendors). so:
- `redwood.experiment_id`
- `redwood.variant_id` (shorter)
- `redwood.experiment_config_version`
Also: route_id should be the normalized template (not raw path) to keep cardinality sane.

Vikram Singh: on the request context propagation: does it survive streaming retries? i remember we had a bug where we re-created ctx for retry path and dropped metadata.

Jada Williams: yeah that’s exactly what i’m worried about. current code sets fields on `RequestContext` and we pass it through `req.Context()` + our internal struct. but the streaming retry is in `streaming/dispatcher.go` and it does:
```go
ctx := context.Background()
ctx = withTrace(ctx, parent)
// TODO: carry RequestContext
```
so it will drop experiment tags. i can patch.

Rafael Mendes: please patch before beta expand. missing attribution on retries will blow up drop-rate alerts and confuse customers. we should also add a unit/integration test that simulates streaming retry and checks tags are present.

Jordan Lee: +1. and for safety: if `RequestContext` is missing on some code path, make sure we set `redwood.experiment_id` to nothing (don’t set “unknown”). “unknown” creates a new label value and screws aggregations.

Caleb Johnson: re: metrics emitters — where do we tag?
- per-request metrics (`inference.request.latency_ms`, `inference.request.error_count`) tagged by `{route_id, model_id, region, experiment_id, variant_id}`
- token metrics are too high volume. we’re not tagging per-token w experiment dims right?

Logan Wright: for GA we agreed minimal set:
- request-level metrics: include experiment dims (required)
- token-level metrics: only include experiment dims in sampled traces, NOT in high-cardinality metrics
this matches the cardinality guidance in the metrics taxonomy notes.

Allison Grant: yep. keep experiment dims off any “by_token” counters/histograms. if we ever add cost/token, it should be derived from request totals (prompt_tokens + completion_tokens) with the experiment dims at request level.

Elena Popov: how are we surfacing assignment to the customer / debugging? are we still returning headers?

Jada Williams: currently:
- response headers always include `x-redwood-experiment-id` + `x-redwood-variant-id` when an experiment applied
- request logs include `experiment_id` and `variant_id`
I’m on the fence about always-on headers vs debug-only.

Rafael Mendes: please keep headers for beta (support needs them), but make it clear they’re informational. also add redaction / allowlist if we think ids leak sensitive info (probably low risk but worth a quick security glance).

Caleb Johnson: one more gotcha: the OTel span tagging happens in `otel/middleware.go` at request start. but experiment assignment happens after routing policy eval (later). so unless we update span attributes mid-request, root span won’t have experiment tags.

Jada Williams: good catch. currently we set attrs on child span (`routing.evaluate`) not the root span. that means dashboards that filter on request root span might miss it.

Allison Grant: we should set on the root span once assignment is known. ok to call `span.SetAttributes(...)` later in the request. just avoid doing it in a tight loop.

Logan Wright: +1. our trace pipeline supports post-hoc attrs. please also set the fields on the log context (structured log fields) from `RequestContext` so even if trace sampling drops, logs still have it.

Jordan Lee: proposal:
- `RequestContext` owns the canonical assignment
- after experiment evaluation, call a helper `observability.SyncExperimentAttrs(ctx)` that:
  - sets attrs on current span (and root if available)
  - updates logger fields
  - updates request-metrics tag set
So we don’t have 3 ad-hoc callsites.

Jada Williams: i like that. action items i’ll take:
1) fix streaming retry path to carry RequestContext (and add integration test)
2) update root span attrs after assignment (not only child span)
3) rename attrs to `redwood.variant_id` and `redwood.experiment_config_version` per Allison’s note
4) ensure we never emit “unknown” label values

Vikram Singh: can you link the exact files once you push? i’ll do a quick review for any other ctx recreation paths (webhooks + async eval hook have similar patterns).

Jada Williams: will do. i’ll open follow-up PR today and tag @Vikram Singh @Caleb Johnson @Allison Grant.

Rafael Mendes: once merged, ping me and i’ll update the oncall notes + attribution drop dashboards to expect retry tagging to improve (so we don’t chase false alarms).

Logan Wright: also please add a small debug log when experiment applied:
`"experiment_assignment", {route_id, experiment_id, variant_id, config_version}`
but only at `DEBUG`.

Jada Williams: ack. thanks all.
