Logan Wright: +1. our trace pipeline supports post-hoc attrs. please also set the fields on the log context (structured log fields) from `RequestContext` so even if trace sampling drops, logs still have it.

Jordan Lee: proposal:
- `RequestContext` owns the canonical assignment
- after experiment evaluation, call a helper `observability.SyncExperimentAttrs(ctx)` that:
  - sets attrs on current span (and root if available)
  - updates logger fields
  - updates request-metrics tag set
So we don’t have 3 ad-hoc callsites.

Jada Williams: i like that. action items i’ll take:
1) fix streaming retry path to carry RequestContext (and add integration test)
2) update root span attrs after assignment (not only child span)
3) rename attrs to `redwood.variant_id` and `redwood.experiment_config_version` per Allison’s note
4) ensure we never emit “unknown” label values

Vikram Singh: can you link the exact files once you push? i’ll do a quick review for any other ctx recreation paths (webhooks + async eval hook have similar patterns).

Jada Williams: will do. i’ll open follow-up PR today and tag @Vikram Singh @Caleb Johnson @Allison Grant.

Rafael Mendes: once merged, ping me and i’ll update the oncall notes + attribution drop dashboards to expect retry tagging to improve (so we don’t chase false alarms).

Logan Wright: also please add a small debug log when experiment applied:
`"experiment_assignment", {route_id, experiment_id, variant_id, config_version}`
but only at `DEBUG`.

Jada Williams: ack. thanks all.

title: #eng-platform: 1736623349
preview:
Jordan Lee: Quick Q we need to settle for route experiments: what’s the precedence if a route is in an active canary rollout *and* an experiment is configured on the same route? (we already have a few internal routes that might do this by accident)

Mina Haddad: +1, we’ll get this from customers too. Canary today is effectively “traffic split stable vs canary target”, and experiment is “traffic split variants”. If we let both run we’re basically double-bucketing.

Ravi Menon: Yeah double hashing = hard to reason about + noisy metrics. Also attribution becomes messy: are we comparing experiment variant deltas or rollout deltas? It’s like 2 independent variables.

Marissa Cole: From Console POV I’d really like a simple story. Either we block it or we have a deterministic rule and we surface a big warning.

Dylan Brooks: UX-wise we can probably block creation when there’s an active rollout on the route (and vice versa), but router still needs a deterministic behavior for “both present” because config propagation can race.

Jada Williams: Let’s enumerate options:
1) Canary first, then experiment (nested). But then you have different experiment populations across stable/canary → impossible to interpret.
2) Experiment first, then canary. Same problem.
3) Disallow both as a product rule; for data plane, pick one winner deterministically and emit a warning metric.

Sean Gallagher: From SRE angle: disallow is the safest. If canary is running, it’s usually because we’re in “change management / rollback ready” mode. An experiment that changes routing/model simultaneously increases blast radius and makes rollback triggers ambiguous.

Hannah Rivera: Also rollback semantics: canary controller may auto-rollback on p99 regression, while experiment guardrails may soft-stop. If both are active, which one “wins”? We can end up with thrash.

Ethan Park: Agree with disallow as the *control plane* policy. But we still need to pick a precedence for the evaluator when both configs exist.

Jordan Lee: For evaluator order, I’m leaning: apply experiment first and treat canary as inactive (for that route) if experiment is active. Rationale: experiment is explicitly “this route is being tested”; if we let canary affect it, the assigned variant can change underneath and we break determinism across time.

Mina Haddad: Counterpoint: canary is also explicit and often higher priority operationally.

Jada Williams: I think we need to differentiate “operator intent” vs “safety”. Safety should always win: hard disables, capacity/fallback, etc. But canary vs experiment is two *traffic shaping* constructs. Product should say “pick one”. For the data plane winner, we should pick the one that keeps assignment deterministic + attribution coherent.

Rafael Mendes: If experiment wins, do we at least log loudly? We’ll want an alertable signal if canary configs are being ignored.

Dylan Brooks: Console can show a banner: “This experiment is overriding an active rollout on the same route” (but ideally we prevent it). If config races still happen, banner + audit event + metric is good.

Sean Gallagher: +1 on metric. Also we should make sure canary controller refuses to start if an active experiment exists (and vice versa) so this doesn’t become a common steady-state.

Marissa Cole: For GA messaging I’m good with “you can’t run an experiment and a canary rollout on the same route at the same time”. Keep it simple.

Ethan Park: Implementation-wise:
- Control plane validation: reject new experiment start if rollout_state(route)=ACTIVE_CANARY.
- Rollouts start API should also reject if experiment_state(route)=RUNNING.
- Router: if both present (stale cache / race), choose one deterministically + annotate request context.

Jordan Lee: What deterministic rule? I’m still on “experiment > canary”, with an `overlap_reason=rollout_present` tag in logs/metrics.

Mina Haddad: If we do that, does it break people who are mid-canary and someone accidentally starts an experiment? That would effectively stop sending traffic to canary.

Jada Williams: That’s why we block in control plane + add RBAC on start. But yes, race case could temporarily drop canary traffic. Alternative is “canary > experiment” so canary continues.

Sean Gallagher: For the race case: I’d rather preserve canary traffic than preserve experiment. Canary is often mitigating risk.

Hannah Rivera: But if canary > experiment, then experiment assignment becomes a no-op for some portion (or all) of traffic, and the experiment results become misleading unless we reflect that in attribution.

Rafael Mendes: Either way, we need explicit reason codes so dashboards don’t lie. Eg `experiment_applied=false reason=rollout_precedence`.

Jordan Lee: Good call. If canary wins and we skip experiment, attribution should not label a variant. If experiment wins and we skip canary, rollout metrics need to see it too.

Jada Williams: Proposal:
- Control plane: enforce mutual exclusion (preferred steady state)
- Data plane precedence (race/legacy): **canary wins** because it’s safety/rollout control
- If canary is active on a route, experiment evaluator returns “not eligible” with reason `blocked_by_rollout` and we emit a counter + attach `experiment_blocked=true` in request context.
This keeps canary semantics intact and avoids silently overriding.

Marissa Cole: I like that. Console can show “Experiment not running because a rollout is active on this route” and link to stop rollout.

Dylan Brooks: +1. That’s a clearer operator story than “your canary got overridden”.

Jordan Lee: I can live with canary > experiment as long as it’s explicit and deterministic. We should still validate at creation/start time so customers don’t get confused.

Ethan Park: Works. We’ll implement precedence in policy engine: Rollout evaluator runs before experiment evaluator for the same route, and sets a flag that blocks experiment evaluation.

Ravi Menon: Edge case: what about “canary” that is basically a rollouts A/B already? Doesn’t matter; we still treat it as rollout.

Sean Gallagher: Please make sure we have an oncall-friendly breadcrumb. A debug endpoint should show: “experiment skipped due to active rollout {rollout_id=...}”.

Rafael Mendes: +1 and add a dashboard panel: “experiment blocked by rollout” count. We can catch misconfigs early.

Jada Williams: Decision summary (please react if disagree):
- ✅ **Product rule:** A route cannot have both an active canary rollout and an active route experiment at the same time. Console + APIs will block starting/creating when the other is active.
- ✅ **Data plane precedence (race/legacy):** **Canary rollout takes precedence over experiment.** If rollout is active, experiment assignment is skipped and marked in request context with `experiment_blocked_reason=blocked_by_rollout`.
- ✅ **Observability:** Emit counters + logs when experiment is blocked by rollout; include rollout_id + experiment_id in debug output (internal only).
- 🔜 **Out of scope:** nested / combined rollout+experiment mode.

Jordan Lee: :thumbsup: I’ll update ENG-9022 notes / evaluator order + add reason code.

Dylan Brooks: :thumbsup: I’ll add UX copy + validation errors (Console wizard + start action).

Rafael Mendes: :thumbsup: I’ll add to runbook + propose an alert threshold if we see this in prod.

Sean Gallagher: :thumbsup: please keep it behind the feature flag until we’ve validated the reason codes show up in traces.

Ethan Park: I’ll coordinate with control plane validation (ENG-9021/9024).

title: #eng-platform: 1736538810
preview:
Jordan Lee: seeing weird allocation skew on route experiments when stickiness key is missing. In staging + 1 beta acct, variant B basically gets ~0-2% even though split is 50/50.

Jordan Lee: suspecting we're hashing `""` or a super low-entropy fallback in the evaluator. can someone from telemetry sanity check whether `stickiness_key_present=false` correlates?

Logan Wright: yep. quick query on staging rollup: when `sticky_key_source=missing` we see almost all requests land in the same bucket.

Logan Wright: pasted from my scratchpad:
```
variant distribution (missing key):
A: 98.7%
B: 1.3%

variant distribution (customer_id):
A: 50.4%
B: 49.6%
```

Ethan Park: :eyes: do we know what the evaluator uses today when missing? last I remember we had precedence order: `x-redwood-sticky-key` -> `end_user_id` -> `customer_id` -> else ???

Jordan Lee: current code path does:
- if none found, `sticky = ""`
- hash("" + experiment_id + salt)

Jordan Lee: so yeah, every request hashes to same value => everyone goes to same variant.

Allison Grant: oof. that also explains why attribution drop didn’t show up; it’s not dropping, it’s just collapsing.

Mina Haddad: we should not silently do that. can we force a deterministic but not-constant fallback?

Jada Williams: product-wise we talked about “safe defaults” but this is *not* safe. if customer forgets to pass key, they think they’re running 50/50 but actually 99/1.

Dylan Brooks: +1, and support will get wrecked. we need Console to warn/block (at least warn loudly) when they configure an experiment without a viable stickiness source.

Benji Okafor: what are options?
1) hard fail experiment start if missing stickiness config / no eligible stable id
2) allow start but default to request_id (non-sticky) so split is correct but users can bounce variants
3) allow start with warning + fixed variant?? (pls no)

Jordan Lee: I’m leaning (2) in router/evaluator: if no stable id, use `request_id` (or trace_id) as entropy so distribution is correct. mark assignment as `non_sticky=true`.

Allison Grant: if we use request_id, at least p99/cost guardrails reflect real distribution. downside is per-user experience (flip-flopping). but that’s better than lying.

Rafael Mendes: from SRE perspective, also want a metric so we can alert if `sticky_key_missing_rate` spikes. otherwise we’ll keep rediscovering this.

Logan Wright: we can emit a counter or tag on span: `experiment.sticky_key_source` with limited cardinality (customer_id/user_id/header/missing). already aligned with ADR-048 naming.

Ethan Park: one more nuance: determinism across retries/streaming. if we use request_id, retries might generate new IDs and re-bucket mid-flight.

Jordan Lee: good call. we can use the gateway request id that’s stable across retries (we already have `x-redwood-request-id`). for streaming retries, I think it’s preserved (but we had that bug last week).

Logan Wright: in the streaming retry bug, the request context was being rebuilt and dropped experiment fields. but request-id header was preserved. so fallback entropy could still be consistent.

Jada Williams: can we also add a Console-side validation warning: “No stickiness key detected for this route; traffic will be assigned per-request and users may see variant changes. Recommended: pass `x-redwood-sticky-key` or `end_user_id`.”

Dylan Brooks: yep. we can implement as:
- creation wizard: highlight “Assignment” step
- show “Detected sources” based on selected route auth mode? (we can at least educate)
- block start only if split < X? jk. but we should block *if* they explicitly choose “sticky by end_user_id” and we can’t find it.

Mina Haddad: we should also consider when customer_id exists but is low-cardinality (single tenant). if they’re single-tenant, customer_id results in constant too. they need end_user_id.

Allison Grant: +1. we should distinguish “present but low entropy” vs missing.

Jordan Lee: entropy detection seems harder. but we can do a heuristic in Console: if selected stickiness is `customer_id` AND account has only one customer_id, warn.

Ethan Park: let’s keep initial fix narrow:
- evaluator: no more empty string fallback. use request_id (or random if missing request_id, but then not deterministic).
- telemetry: emit `sticky_key_source` + `sticky_key_missing` metric
- Console: warning copy + doc link

Rafael Mendes: also add a guardrail banner in experiment details when `sticky_key_source=missing` > e.g. 5% over last 15m.

Dylan Brooks: that’s great. we can put it next to “Attribution drop rate” and “Propagation lag” banners.

Logan Wright: for alerting, I’ll add to dashboard spec: panel “% assignments with missing sticky key (by route, experiment)” with thresholds.

Jordan Lee: I’ll push a PR to change fallback to:
```
if sticky == "": sticky = request_id
if still empty: sticky = trace_id
if still empty: sticky = random_uuid (logged)
```
plus set `assignment_mode=per_request` when we’re not using a stable id.

Allison Grant: please be careful with tags/cardinality. `assignment_mode` is fine. don’t tag raw source ids.

Ethan Park: +1. and make sure we don’t break determinism across regions (shouldn’t, since request-id is per request anyway).

Jada Williams: what about eligibility rules that rely on user_id? if missing user_id, they may become “ineligible” and experiment doesn’t apply, which could hide the problem.

Jordan Lee: today, eligibility is separate from stickiness. If they use “all traffic” eligibility, experiment applies even without user_id.

Jordan Lee: but if they use rule `end_user_id exists`, then missing => not eligible, which is correct.

Dylan Brooks: Console should clarify this: “Eligibility: requires end_user_id” vs “Assignment key: end_user_id”. customers mix those up.

Benji Okafor: I can add a server-side validation warning in config API response too. even if UI misses it, API callers see it.

Ethan Park: ok. action items:
- Jordan: evaluator fix + unit test (missing sticky key should not collapse split)
- Logan: add missing-key metrics + dashboard panel
- Dylan/Jada: Console validation warning + copy + docs link
- Benji: API validation warnings + surface in start response

Rafael Mendes: and please add a note to runbook: “variant skew often indicates missing stickiness key causing constant hash.”

Jordan Lee: ack. I’ll link the PR here when up.

Jordan Lee: PR up: `pr-28705-fix-variant-skew-when-stickiness-key-missing` (WIP). adds fallback to request-id + sets `assignment_mode=per_request` when using fallback. tests include distribution check over 10k synthetic requests.

Allison Grant: reviewed quickly. looks good. can we also emit a structured log once per minute when fallback mode is used > N times? helps oncall.

Jordan Lee: added a rate-limited log: `experiment_assignment_fallback_used` with fields `{route_id, experiment_id, reason=missing_sticky_key}`.

Logan Wright: :thumbsup: once merged, I’ll update the rollup job to include `assignment_mode` as a dimension (2 values only) and wire to Console banner.

Dylan Brooks: for UI, we’ll add in the wizard:
- “Stickiness key” selector default: “Recommended: end_user_id (if provided)” else “customer_id”
- warning callout if we can’t guarantee stable stickiness
- tooltip linking to SDK docs for `x-redwood-sticky-key`

Jada Williams: also add a warning on experiment details if distribution is wildly off vs configured split (e.g. abs(delta) > 20%). not perfect but helpful.

Rafael Mendes: careful w false positives on small traffic. maybe gate that on min sample size.

Ethan Park: agree. start with missing-key banner + logging; skew detection can be follow-up.

Jordan Lee: merged fix to main. rollout behind feature flag `route_experiments_safe_fallback` default ON in staging, then beta.

Logan Wright: I’ll monitor staging tonight. if we see `missing` drop and distribution normalize, we can enable for beta accts.

Dylan Brooks: thanks all. support team has already pinged about “why is variant header always A” so this should help a lot.

Dylan Brooks: Console should clarify this: “Eligibility: requires end_user_id” vs “Assignment key: end_user_id”. customers mix those up.

Benji Okafor: I can add a server-side validation warning in config API response too. even if UI misses it, API callers see it.

Ethan Park: ok. action items:
- Jordan: evaluator fix + unit test (missing sticky key should not collapse split)
- Logan: add missing-key metrics + dashboard panel
- Dylan/Jada: Console validation warning + copy + docs link
- Benji: API validation warnings + surface in start response

Rafael Mendes: and please add a note to runbook: “variant skew often indicates missing stickiness key causing constant hash.”

Jordan Lee: ack. I’ll link the PR here when up.

Jordan Lee: PR up: `pr-28705-fix-variant-skew-when-stickiness-key-missing` (WIP). adds fallback to request-id + sets `assignment_mode=per_request` when using fallback. tests include distribution check over 10k synthetic requests.

Allison Grant: reviewed quickly. looks good. can we also emit a structured log once per minute when fallback mode is used > N times? helps oncall.

Jordan Lee: added a rate-limited log: `experiment_assignment_fallback_used` with fields `{route_id, experiment_id, reason=missing_sticky_key}`.

Logan Wright: :thumbsup: once merged, I’ll update the rollup job to include `assignment_mode` as a dimension (2 values only) and wire to Console banner.

Dylan Brooks: for UI, we’ll add in the wizard:
- “Stickiness key” selector default: “Recommended: end_user_id (if provided)” else “customer_id”
- warning callout if we can’t guarantee stable stickiness
- tooltip linking to SDK docs for `x-redwood-sticky-key`

Jada Williams: also add a warning on experiment details if distribution is wildly off vs configured split (e.g. abs(delta) > 20%). not perfect but helpful.

Rafael Mendes: careful w false positives on small traffic. maybe gate that on min sample size.

Ethan Park: agree. start with missing-key banner + logging; skew detection can be follow-up.

Jordan Lee: merged fix to main. rollout behind feature flag `route_experiments_safe_fallback` default ON in staging, then beta.

Logan Wright: I’ll monitor staging tonight. if we see `missing` drop and distribution normalize, we can enable for beta accts.

Dylan Brooks: thanks all. support team has already pinged about “why is variant header always A” so this should help a lot.

title: #eng-platform: 1736039442
preview:
Ethan Park: seeing weird config propagation lag in staging for route experiments. console says experiment started but router still serving control for ~6-10 mins. anyone else noticing?

Benji Okafor: yeah, hit it twice today. `experiment-service` shows state=RUNNING + config_version incremented, but policy engine evaluator still pulling old snapshot.

Jordan Lee: +1. I was debugging split skew and noticed the evaluator using `config_version=118` while control plane already at `124`.

Mina Haddad: is this only staging? prod seems ok (but lower churn).

Rafael Mendes: if lag is real we should get ahead of it before beta expansion. do we have a metric for “config staleness” at router/policy layer?

Michael Nguyen: we have `config_cache_hit` and `config_refresh_ms` but nothing directly comparing expected version vs observed version.

Ethan Park: dropping some logs. from experiment-service (staging):
```
2025-01-03T18:07:11.903Z INFO experiment.update actor=marissa_cole exp_id=exp_7f3c route_id=rt_204 action=START from=DRAFT to=RUNNING config_version=124
2025-01-03T18:07:12.014Z INFO publish.config_update topic=experiment-config-updates key=rt_204 version=124
```
policy-engine (staging) ~8 mins later:
```
2025-01-03T18:15:41.227Z DEBUG evaluator.load route=rt_204 cache=HIT version=118 age_s=523
2025-01-03T18:15:41.229Z DEBUG evaluator.decision route=rt_204 exp=exp_7f3c variant=control reason="no_active_experiment_in_snapshot"
```

Caleb Johnson: quick q: evaluator cache hit suggests it never refreshed. do we rely on pubsub invalidation in staging or periodic poll?

Jordan Lee: both. default is pubsub invalidation + periodic 60s poll as backstop. but that log shows `cache=HIT` and age_s > 500 which shouldn’t happen if poll is working.

Logan Wright: from telemetry side: I’m seeing “results not updating” complaints because attribution dims do show exp_id/variant_id only after router starts applying it. dashboard looks flat until then.

Sean Gallagher: do we know if this is “config not delivered” vs “delivered but not applied”? i.e. could be router not reloading policy engine bundle.

Vikram Singh: router pulls policy snapshot from policy-engine via internal gRPC. if evaluator hasn’t refreshed, router won’t see it. so likely upstream.

Amara Diallo: any chance staging pubsub is dropping messages? we had flaky nats in staging last week.

Michael Nguyen: we should check subscriber lag + drop counters on `experiment-config-updates` in staging.

Allison Grant: +1. also want to make sure we aren’t tagging metrics with raw route path creating cardinality issues, but separate topic.

Ethan Park: hypothesis list so far:
1) pubsub subscriber not connected / lagging
2) evaluator poller thread stuck / dead
3) cache TTL misconfigured in staging (too long) OR timestamp bug
4) config update published but filtered due to route_id normalization mismatch (rt_204 vs /v1/chat/...)

Jordan Lee: #4 is real possibility. we recently changed route-id normalization rules (template vs raw) in staging? i saw a patch in routing framework.

Benji Okafor: experiment-service publishes by `route_id` (internal id), so mismatch would have to be in evaluator subscription keying.

Caleb Johnson: can someone run a quick “explain” on staging using the debug endpoint? if it prints config version + last refresh time.

Jordan Lee: did that. output:
```
GET /internal/experiments/debug/route/rt_204
{
  "route_id": "rt_204",
  "snapshot_version": 118,
  "snapshot_loaded_at": "2025-01-03T18:06:58Z",
  "last_invalidation_event": null,
  "poller_last_run": "2025-01-03T18:07:00Z",
  "poller_status": "stopped" 
}
```
Poller stopped :grimacing:

Sean Gallagher: ok that’s the smoking gun. why is poller stopped? panic? thread crash? OOM?

Jordan Lee: checking earlier logs:
```
2025-01-03T18:07:00.112Z ERROR config.poller failed err="context deadline exceeded" target="experiment-service:443" attempt=3
2025-01-03T18:07:00.112Z ERROR config.poller entering_backoff backoff_s=600
```
so it’s not “stopped”, it’s in 10 min backoff. debug endpoint labels it poorly.

Rafael Mendes: 10 min backoff on staging explains the 6-10 min lag. but why are we timing out to experiment-service? that’s in same cluster.

Michael Nguyen: is experiment-service behind auth in staging? could be token refresh failing causing retries.

Benji Okafor: experiment-service p99 for `GetActiveConfig` in staging spiked earlier.
```
2025-01-03T18:06:59Z p99=3.8s p95=1.9s errors=0
```
client timeout on policy side might be 2s.

Ethan Park: yeah, policy-engine uses 2s deadline (copied from other config sources). staging DB was under load due to migration tests.

Sean Gallagher: action: reduce blast radius by not backing off for 10 mins on a single timeout. we need: bounded staleness and faster recovery.

Allison Grant: +1. and metric/alerting: “config propagation lag” measured end-to-end.

Logan Wright: metrics proposal (minimal):
- `experiment_config_snapshot_age_seconds{service=policy-engine}` (now - snapshot_loaded_at)
- `experiment_config_fetch_latency_ms{service=policy-engine}`
- `experiment_config_fetch_errors_total{code}`
- `experiment_config_version{service=experiment-service}` as gauge? maybe too expensive.

Caleb Johnson: gauge for version is tricky; better is compute “lag” via embedding `config_version` in event + in evaluator snapshot; emit a delta:
- `experiment_config_version_lag{route_id?}` but route_id tagging could explode.

Allison Grant: do NOT tag by route_id in metrics. maybe sample top routes or emit only aggregate percentiles. we can do logs/traces for per-route.

Rafael Mendes: agreed. for alerts we want global + maybe per-region.
Suggested alerts:
- snapshot_age p95 > 120s for 10m (warning)
- snapshot_age p95 > 300s for 5m (page?)
- invalidation drop rate > X%

Jordan Lee: also propose a dedicated metric on router side: when request is evaluated, include `policy_snapshot_age_s` as span attribute. can build histogram from traces.

Caleb Johnson: span attr is fine; but for metrics pipeline we should have an explicit histogram in policy-engine itself.

Ethan Park: near-term fix ideas:
1) decrease backoff cap from 600s -> 30s and keep polling
2) raise deadline from 2s -> 5s OR make it adaptive
3) add circuit breaker so we don’t DOS experiment-service when it’s slow

Benji Okafor: #1 feels safest for now. 10 min is too aggressive.

Sean Gallagher: caution: don’t remove backoff entirely; if downstream is hard down we’ll spin. but 10 min staleness is unacceptable for “start/stop” semantics.

Mina Haddad: can we also use pubsub invalidation more reliably? if event delivered, evaluator can refresh without waiting for poll.

Michael Nguyen: we still need poll as backstop (pubsub can drop). but we can change backoff logic: invalidation should immediately trigger refresh attempt even during backoff.

Jordan Lee: yes. current code uses a shared limiter; backoff blocks both poll + invalidation refresh. that’s likely the root design issue.

DeployBot: buildkite #staging-policy-engine build 8821 failed (unit tests). link: https://buildkite.redwood.example/policy-engine/builds/8821

Ethan Park: ok, who can take the code change? Jordan?

Jordan Lee: I can patch evaluator to separate backoff domains: fetch failures backoff only affects polling, invalidations still enqueue refresh with independent short jitter. plus we’ll rename debug endpoint fields to not say “stopped”.

Rafael Mendes: please also add the metrics we discussed. at minimum `snapshot_age_seconds` and `refresh_attempts_total{result}`.

Logan Wright: telemetry can help add a derived “propagation lag” metric computed as (now - config_version_created_at) if we carry `created_at` in config payload. do we have that?

Benji Okafor: config object has `updated_at` already. we can include it in evaluator snapshot and compute lag without needing version gauge.

Allison Grant: perfect. Metric definition:
- `experiment_config_propagation_lag_seconds` = now - snapshot.updated_at
No route_id labels. labels: env, region, service.

Sean Gallagher: add dashboard panels + alert rules draft. also add a runbook link once we have it.

Ethan Park: next steps:
- Jordan: PR for evaluator backoff + metrics
- Benji/Michael: check experiment-service staging latency + DB load; confirm if anything else causing timeouts
- Logan/Caleb/Allison: align on metric names + OTel tags (keep low cardinality)
- Rafael: propose alert thresholds; we’ll tune after a day of data

Jordan Lee: sounds good. I’ll open a PR and reference this thread.

Benji Okafor: I’ll look at experiment-service slow traces around 18:06-18:08.

Sean Gallagher: ping me when metric exists; I want it on the SRE “beta readiness” checklist.

Ethan Park: thanks all. will update when PR is up.
